sentences.text: And deportations-- in 1942, after we were married already, on Rosh Hashana.
We were married in July 1942, and on Rosh Hashana 1942, there was another deportation.
At that time, my husband already got to be the organizer of a company which was called Madritsch.
So I got a job by Madritsch.
We made uniforms and civil clothes by Madritsch.
It was out-- they formed the ghetto already-- and it was outside from the ghetto.
We were taken every day from our apartment, which was in the ghetto, to the working place by Madritsch outside the ghetto.
And now, there was another deportation coming up.

And my husband knew-- a friend of his had a company outside from the ghetto in a little-- like a little house, it was, and it had an upstairs like-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like a little-- the upstairs, what is-- what can it be called?
There was one magazine, like a-- like a-- like a store, and then there was, on top, there was another little thing to sit on.
And then there was a basement, which was deep, very deep, only dirt, like.
But we went into this little house-- it was Rosh Hashana-- with one seder.
And we took in some water, and we took in some bread, and some sugar-- sugar cubes.
We were-- I don't know how many people, we were a lot of people there, but not as many as the next time we were hiding.
But anyhow, my husband will tell you this story of how it-- how I came out again from there.
Anyhow, we were sitting there.
And there were some windows where we were sitting, and-- and with the seder and davening, and the Polish little boys were playing [INAUDIBLE] there, because it was outside from the ghetto.
And I saw them, and I said, oh my goodness, we are lost, because they saw us.
But the sun was blinding them, so they couldn't have seen us.
But we were sitting then.
The next day, they were screaming.
There were some other bunkers outside from the ghetto it was.
They were on the other street, and they were screaming, raus, raus, raus, out, out, out.
So I said to my husband, there's no way that we will be safe, anyhow.
Let's go out.
For the first time, I gave up, and I wanted to go out.
And my husband said, when they're coming here, and they take us out from here, then we go out.
Not-- not this way.
So what we did was, we went deeper into another-- into another basement, which was only dirt.
We had a candle, and we wanted to light a candle.
It was so terribly dark.
And we couldn't because the moisture was.
Then we sat there till-- till the last day.
Then a Jewish policeman, who had to take us back-- my husband will tell you how it was.
He wanted to take out his-- his mother and not his wife, because he loved his mother very much-- not his wife.
But anyhow, so he wanted to-- he took us out.
And he had to go at 2 o'clock in the morning.
He took us out from there, back into the ghetto.
It was dangerous, because the Gestapo and the SS were all over.
So we were walk in understockings, and we went back into the ghetto.
Now, I came into the ghetto.
[CRYING] The Gestapo came in again, and I had to go into hiding with my cousins where I went, because I couldn't go into our apartment anymore.
It was like a ghost town.
It was completely like-- I didn't look in the mirror, but everybody told me I looked like I came from the grave after the eight days.
So we were saved again.
Now we worked for Madritsch [CRYING] till Goeth came-- Goeth came home.
[CRYING] [DRINKS] Madritsch and Goeth-- Goeth was the oberscharfuhrer from Plaszow in Krakow, where all the Krakow people worked already.
Now Madritsch wanted to open up a company there, in-- in-- in Plaszow.
So because the ghetto had to be liquidated-- they had two ghettos in the meantime, an A ghetto and a B ghetto.
We worked--we were the working ghetto.
And then there was the A ghetto for the-- for the-- for the old people and for the children.

So we had to-- the 1st of-- the 2nd of September, my husband's birthday, I remember that-- my husband's birthday, at 6 o'clock in the morning, the ghetto was-- everybody was-- like-- they came, and we had to go out to the appellplatz there.
And Goeth came, and he had everything for us by companies.
We were working for Madritsch, and the ghetto was supposed to be liquidated.
So we were taken out to the trains, to the-- you know, to the cattle cars.
And we're taking 105 people in one car.
We were taken to Plaszow.
And when we came to Plaszow, it was just in the evening.
And there were big, big-- we didn't eat anything from the morning, 6 o'clock, til the next evening, we didn't have anything.
We were standing on the appellplatz till they took us out.
The next day, by-- it was by 1 o'clock until we got in there, it was in the evening, we didn't have anything to drink, not a drop of water-- and it's-- nothing to eat.
But anyhow, this wasn't the worst thing.
The worst thing was, that we saw my sister-in-law, who was with the kids.
She was walking by and she said, you see, I'm going to Auschwitz.
They knew that this is the end.
I mean, she took the kids, and they-- and the little boys, they wanted want to go.
My brother-in-law, at that time, he came into our group from Madritsch.
So he was safe.
But his boys wanted to get into our group.
They were 11 and 13 years old.
I don't remember exactly their ages.
But they wanted to come to our group, to Madritsch.
And Goeth chased them out.
And my husband will tell you, the third time, he said, I'm going to shoot you.
So they had to go with their mother to Auschwitz to be gassed.
So that's the last time I saw them.
Then we came to Plaszow, and we worked for Madritsch.
We were very lucky-- my husband and me, we both got day shift, which meant we went into the magazine, where they were-- the ready-made clothes came in there.
And I had to make out all the office work, like the [NON-ENGLISH] it was called-- you know, how much goes out, and what kind of article it is, and things like that.
So we had the day shift, which was very good.
And the lageralteste, the Jewish lageralteste, his sister worked with me, too.
Now, we worked there for-- for very many months.
And finally, they liquidated-- they wanted to-- the Russian front came forwards, and they wanted to liquidate Plaszow, too.
So they took 8,000 women.
I was one of-- with the 8,000 women.
And they took them-- they wanted to take me, too-- to Stutthof.
And a Jewish OD man, who knew me very well, from-- from Tarnow, he was-- he was working in Tarnow and then came to Krakow too, with us, so he knew me.
And my husband actually asked the woman I worked with, who was the sister of the Jewish lagerfuhrer, Hillevich, sister, Mrs.
Schnitzer was her name, Fela Schnitzer.
She was a very good friend-- I mean, she seemed to be friendly to me, but when my husband asked her, can your brother take my wife out from the transport, she said, no.
We cannot do anything.
And I saw all these people who were taken out who were working with the Jewish police.
But the Jewish policeman came with water, and the SS chased him away.
But he said to me, these two words, go in the back.
They don't have enough room for everybody.
And I did that.
And I came back to Plaszow to the camp.
And I was saved again.
Because I worked a little while more, too, for Madritsch.
And then Madritsch and Schindler were friends, too.
And Schindler said to Madritsch, how about coming with me?
I'm going to make a factory in Brunnlitz, in Czechoslovakia.
But Madritsch didn't want anymore.
Maybe he wasn't able to do it because he had enough money made already on the Jewish workers, so he said he's not interested.
But Schindler made the factory.
Now, Mr.
Tisch, who was a wonderful-- he was a German, but he was-- I don't believe he was even a Nazi-- but he worked-- there he was, an [NON-ENGLISH], he was the manager of Madritsch in Krakow.
So he was--he knew me, because we had some contact.
You know, he came into the magazine.
And he said one day, there's a-- there's a-- a transport going to Brunnlitz And I said, oh can I go, can I go?
Because every-- every time there was a transport, I never volunteered to go no place, because I knew it could only be worse, not-- not better.
Because I didn't know where I will even make it, you know.
So I always refused.
I'd never officially volunteered for anything.
But this, I volunteered.
Because I knew that, here there is no chance to live at all, and maybe there will be a chance.
And I believed in Mr.
Tisch, because he was really honest-looking to me.
He had an honest face.
And so he said, OK, I can put you on the list.
And I said, I have a husband and a brother-in-law.
He put them on the list, too-- who were working for Madritsch, too.
So we came on the list to Schindler.
And Schindler, I never met before.
And so, for the first time, when we came, of course, when the list was made, there was a lot of turmoil, and everybody wanted to be on the list.
And everybody wanted to go there.
And then I knew, maybe it's good.
So but then, we were only 60 or 70 people altogether, for Madritsch, taken on Schindler's List.
And it the list was not touched.
For one reason-- because they did not want to have anything done to Tisch.
Because they knew Tisch's word was very, very important.
So we were on the list to go to Brunnlitz.
Oh, Plaszow was terrible.
I can tell you about Plaszow.
In Plaszow, first of all, I got once very sick.
I had a very high fever, and I went into the krankenstube.
Now, as sick as I was, I didn't want to stay there, because they-- they knew that, God forbid, if you stayed there a little longer, you might get to Auschwitz, they would take you out anyhow.
One day, it was a very, very, a cold day.
It was in May.
And Mengele-- Dr.
Mengele from-- from Auschwitz came, with some other guys, and they had tables set up.
And we had stand there naked.
All-- everybody was naked.
And they had like files with paper.
And everybody had to march down the aisles.
And Mengele was looking you over.
I was lucky, I wasn't on the-- I mean, they did not take my name.
But if he saw somebody who was a little fragile looking, or so, they just put the number on it.
We had numbers.
And the next day they came and they took them to Auschwitz.
That was in Plaszow.
Another thing was in Plaszow, one day they all called us down.
And there were big loudspeakers in Plaszow, on-- on the appellplatz.
And they were-- the children who were saved by Schindler-- because he had a factory outside from Plaszow for a while in Krakow-- and these kids were saved.
There were maybe by 300 children or so.
There was a big truck.
And the truck came and took all the children away.
And they were singing a lullaby on the loudspeaker-- good evening, goodnight.
In German, of course.
If God wants you to, you will be awaking again.
So that was a terrible day for us, to really sort of-- the parents wanted to go with the children.
Of course, they couldn't go.
I was so lucky I didn't have children.
I would have committed suicide when I saw that.
[SOBBING] And my husband will tell you about somebody who was hiding, and they found him.
And there were hangings all the time in Plaszow.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now the lagerfuhrer had a sister I told you about, who worked with me together.
One morning, she came from the latrine, which is still-- you know, it was like-- a little house.
Where there were only holes, like you went to the latrine.
When you saw the movie, this little boy was hiding there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So Goeth came.
And we just came back.
I saw him coming, and he said, to the ordnungsdienst-- her husband was an ordnungsdienst.
He said, call your wife out.
And I was just going in.
He called his wife out.
And I didn't know what was going on.
But she was shot in front of me-- in front of where we worked, because she worked with me together.

What was happening was, that there-- Goeth was black marketing with this lagerfuhrer-- the Jewish lagerfuhrer-- and I guess when we came from town of after make revision-- they cut out all the clothes, you know, to find some diamonds and things like this.
And I guess he had something to do with Goeth, too, and he knew too much.
But Goeth made a thing out of it, that he like, said they wanted to run away.
Or the big shots, the administration, the Jewish administration.
So he wants the sister of the Jewish lagerfuhrer-- so she was between them.
So they shot Goeth, and they shot-- they shot-- I'm sorry.
Goeth took out Hillevich, who was the lagerfuhrer.
He took out his wife, Mrs.
Hillevich, and he took out this woman, who was a sister to him, who worked with me together, Fela Schnitzer, and her husband, and a brother-- brother-in-law of Mr.
Hillevich.
There were eight people-- the big shots.
They were all shot.
And we had to walk past by, the whole camp.
And there was a big sign, because they want to [NON-ENGLISH], which means they wanted to run away.
And they had jewelry or something hidden, or something like this.
He wanted them out of the picture, because it was already almost the end of the Plaszow ghetto, the liquidation-- you know, when we were taken there.
This was Plaszow.
Every day there was something new.
You know what I had?
I had a condom still from Tarnow.
And my husband, he will tell you about that.
He had some dollars, and I had some-- some dollars in the condom.
In-- in Auschwitz, when I came to Auschwitz, they said they're going to x-ray us.
Now, I couldn't care less whether I died there, because I didn't believe in going to Brunnlitz-- they will take us to Brunnlitz.
I said, so they shoot me with the condom inside.
I had a condom with me, inside, all the time.
I had to eat with that.
I had to sleep with that.
I had some dollars there, which I-- thank God.
We came back, the first $50, we had something to eat, we had something to buy.
So that's the way I had to live.
You can imagine.
With a condom inside of me-- I had some $50 there.
And it was a lot of money at that time, because it was, you know.
And my husband had some money too.
I don't know where-- he was hiding it too.
In the camp?
We were living in different barracks.
You know, we had different barracks.
But during the time we worked, we worked together in the same magazine, in which we saw each other.
Communicate, yes.
We-- we-- we did.
Yeah.
In Plaszow we were from 1930-- I have to look.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] From 1933 to October 1942-- '44.
Yeah.
And then you were supposed to go to Brunnlitz.
And then we were supposed to go to Brunnlitz.
Now, instead of taking us to Brunnlitz, they took us to Auschwitz.
And again, you know, we were like naked again.
They gave us wooden shoes like that.
They're called Hollanders-- you know, they're like the Holland people wear.
But there was one Jewish guy who was-- when we came to Auschwitz it was just 3 o'clock in the morning.
And everything was fast, fast, fast.
And we had to come into a very big room.
And we saw just Jewish boys going by.
And-- and, you know, they had like caps on, and they had like the striped uniforms.
And we asked, you know, what will be with us.
And they said-- like this.
You know, they made like, a sign that we were very, very, in the wrong place.
And then the next day, they took us to the-- to the-- to the zone there, to the-- to the bus, thing-- naked, of course.
And we were-- there were all the people.
All the people who were still being in Plaszow, we were all taken there.
And our transport wasn't even honored, because when we came to Auschwitz, I had a very bad incident.
Mengele was there.
And he had another doctor there.
And another woman who worked with me had a daughter who was 15 years old.
So she was standing in front.
We were always in fifths, you know, fifths-- one, two, three, four, five, in rows.
And she was standing in front, and I was standing the second.
And her mother was standing behind the third-- we came out like this.
You know, we were frightened all the time, so you did whatever they told you to do.
So she said to me, Margot, be so kind, maybe you can switch with her.
You know, I was already old, I was 24.
So-- so I went and I switched.
And he saw that, Mengele.
And he took out already three women before that.
You know, towards the side, towards where the oven was.
So I said, I can-- in German.
I spoke to him.
He took me out, too, because I did that.
You know, I got very frightened, and very pale.
And I said, I'm young, I can still work, and so.
And there was another doctor.
So he said, let her go back.
So I went back into the row.
Otherwise, I would have been taken there, too, already, in Auschwitz.
And then, of course, you know, there were selections all the time in Auschwitz.
They came into the barrack, and Mengele, and we were naked, and it was a terrible time, too.
But at least, then, after three or four weeks, they came, and they called the names.
Now we were not numbers anymore.
They called us by names.
They called the 300 women by name.
In the meantime, I was in Auschwitz, and the ovens were burning day and night.
In Birkenau, it was-- Birkenau it was.
The women's concentration camp.
And the ovens were going day and night.
And I met a cousin.
A cousin-- my-- my-- actually, she was not my cousin by birth.
Her fiance was a cousin who lived with us in Tarnow.
And he wanted to save himself, so he took a farmer of somebody, and he was supposed to take them out to-- to his house.
In the meantime, he had the money, and he took him to the Gestapo, so he was killed, the cousin of mine.
So she has fiance.
And she was taken from another town where she lived with her parents.
And from [NON-ENGLISH]-- not from [NON-ENGLISH], from a town next to Auschwitz.
And they were all gassed.
So she was all by herself.
Now, she was all by herself, and then she saw my name on-- on the list-- you know, she got to see the list-- she said to me, oh, I'm your cousin.
And I said, oh, yeah, you're Hania Viener, I only heard about her, but I didn't know her.
And when they came afterwards, to pick us out, to call our name, I said to the [NON-ENGLISH] there, who took us out, he was-- I was telling him, in German, I met a cousin, can she go with me?
And not that I knew that it's safe, but somehow I wanted to stay with her, and he said, OK.
So she went with me to Brunnlitz, and she lived through the war.
And she has two sons-- a son and a daughter, and they have, between the two, they have 11 grandchildren.
But she is very sick.
She doesn't want to go to see the movie.
She doesn't want to know anything.
[CRYING]
Yeah, yeah-- OK.
Yeah, yeah.
Yeah.
I tell you, I-- I knew right away-- that when we were in the-- in the train, you know what I did?
I thanked God.
I go to die, I know.
But my-- my-- my brothers will be alive.
And that's what I did.
I knew I go to [CRYING].
That's why I thanked God.
I didn't think about myself.
I was numb.
When we came to Brunnlitz, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, the fabric-- the factory-- had chimneys.
And there was Pfefferberg, Mila Pfefferberg, Poldek Pfefferberg, who actually told Keneally to write the book.
She was a friend of mine there.
And I was next to her, and I said, Mila, I'm running away.
So she said-- they're burning us here, because I saw the chimneys going again, you know.
So she said, Margot, if they wanted to burn us, they would have gassed us in Auschwitz.
So actually, when we came there, the men were standing and waiting for us.
I saw Mr.
Schindler for the first time.
And he came down, and he said to us-- you're safe here with us.
And there's no more shooting.
And you can imagine how we felt.
Even though we slept on the straw, there were no-- [CRYING] there were no britches like in Auschwitz-- you know, they don't do anything, nothing.
We slept on the straw, but it was fine, because we knew that we are safe.
[CRYING]
So we made the shells.
And we polished the shells in Brunnlitz.
And Mr.
Schindler walked by sometimes.
You know, and Mrs.
Schindler sometimes-- she was a beautiful woman.
And gentle-looking woman.
But one time, when-- when Roosevelt died, one time she made a remark which wasn't very nice, and I couldn't forget it.
She said, the Judenfater died.
And he wasn't really the Judenfater, because he didn't let the Jewish people come in, either.
But that's, you know, that's another story.
But that story, it was-- we saw Schindler, and for a few days, there were SS women, too.
One SS woman, got to-- she got to like me a little bit, or something, I don't know.
And she told me that I should watch, during-- during lunchtime, when we got a little bit of soup, the men and the women, they started to talk to each other.
So she wanted me to tell them they cannot do that.
So I said, no, I cannot do that-- and I could get some bread or something more of something.
And I said, I cannot do it, because I like myself to talk to my husband.
I wouldn't like to be a cop or something like that.
That was-- Were you able to speak with your husband much?
Yeah, we could speak, yes, we could speak during-- when he was outside, and I was inside, you know.
And there, I had to work night shift, too.
So we were in the bunder-- which means-- it was like a big, big kettle with-- with acid.
And the-- the shells had to be boiled there before they went out to transport.
So we had to do that.
And it was smelling terrible there.
The acid went into the-- you respiratory system.
And I got a very bad eye infection from it.
And to this day, I'm blind in one eye.
I can hardly see in one eye.
It was never cured.
It was never done.
And other things I had, too, from all the acid, and from all these things.
But thank God for that.
We were cleaning the shells before they were made, and then we were boiling the shells.
And then we had to take them out to dry them, and things like that.
Instructions?
What kind of, what do you--
To do my work-- there was one foreman, like, you know, another German, a Jewish woman, and she just said what we have to do.
The eating conditions?
We got in the morning, we got a piece of bread from-- from a big quarter of something, a piece of bread.
And then we got soup.
Which was better than in-- in-- in-- better than in Auschwitz, you know.
And we got some margarine sometimes.
And when Hitler-- when Hitler-- when Schindler had the first day, we got a little bit of farina and white bread.
Yeah.
And one-- one girl, I'll never forget that-- she had bone cancer.
And she was dying, actually.
And Mrs.
Schindler took her into her quarters, there, and she spoon-fed her farina.
This I heard, yes.
She was maybe 17 or 18 years old.
Her name was-- it's in the book, in our book.
In the book, which was written.
Her whole story is there, too.
In Brunnlitz?
We were all young.
I mean, 25, 24.
Some of them were older, because they came from-- from the list from-- from Schindler, from Krakow.
You know, they were young and old, and even young kids.
As a matter of fact, there were young boys who when-- when Schindler was not there, they were working in the factory there.
And there was another guy, who came from another camp.
He took him to Auschwitz, back to Auschwitz.
So the parents-- Rosen was their name.
Rosen and Horowitz.
It's in the book there, too, if you read the book.
So anyhow, their stories are in there too.
So they were taken back to-- to Auschwitz.
And we worked by-- by, in Brunnlitz, we worked there until 1945, May the 9th, 1945.
And then a guy on a horse came, a Russian guy, and he said you are free.
And I said, this can't be.
It was impossible.
And then I-- they told us not to go out, not to walk out from the camp, because they were shooting all over.
The Germans were running away, and, of course, Schindler went away, and we were all by ourselves there in the camp.
And they said we had to clean the camp now.
And I just wanted to see how the outside looks.
I was going out and I said, oh there's a world outside.
You know, I didn't believe that I ever will see daylight.
So I went out, and a German woman who was living next door approached me, and she said, would you like to move into my house?
And I said, I have a husband and a brother-in-law.
They have to come with me.
So she said, OK.
So we moved into that house.
And we were staying.
Then she opened up the kitchen-- she had a-- she was German, she had-- everything was clean.
And she said I should bake a cake if I want.
And we-- you see, Schindler took over a magazine from a Jewish guy who had fabrics.
And Schindler said that whoever leaves when-- when the war is over, they should get some coupons-- some-- some fabrics.
So I had some fabrics, we all got some fabrics.
And I went into the machine, and I made myself a blouse and a skirt-- because I had rags.
I had nothing to wear, you know.
And on my blouse, which I had on-- it wasn't a blouse, it was like a-- I cannot even start to tell you what it was.
It was-- a cross was cut out.
Everybody had to have a cut-out cross to be identified, or something, not to run away.
So anyhow, I made myself a blouse and a skirt, and I put myself together like I was looking a little bit more decent.
So that's the way.
And then after two or three days, we went into the bedroom of this lady.
There was a guy in an SS uniform.
And we asked her who this is.
So she said, that's my husband.
And I don't know where he is-- a German.
So we didn't want to stay there any longer.
There was no transportation going back to town, if we wanted to go to see whether somebody's alive.
I didn't expect that anybody from my family, so much, but my husband's family.
But when we came-- so we went on a-- on a coal train, on a freight train there.
And so we came back to Tarnow, and there was nobody we knew.
A friend of my husband said-- he had already an apartment-- he came a little bit earlier from a camp.
And he said we can move into him.
And we moved into him, and then some other people came to us.
So we were free.
And my husband went to a Polish guy who he had hidden all his fabrics.
He will tell you about how he did that.
And he took it out.
And these people were very upset, because they never believed that this can happen.
So we took out the merchandise from there, and my husband started to sell a little bit.
And that's the way we started.
And then we went to France.
Yeah.
OK.
Yeah, OK.
Good.
